content:
7 min read
Just now

--

I picked the wrong language first. It cost me four months I didn’t need to lose.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The language you start with shapes whether you stick around long enough to get good.
I started learning to code with Java, because a forum post from 2014 told me it would “teach me real programming discipline.”
Three weeks in, I was still trying to understand why I needed to write twelve lines of boilerplate just to print “Hello World” to a screen. I wasn’t learning to think like a programmer. I was learning to fight a compiler. By month four, I’d quietly given up, telling myself coding “wasn’t really for me.”
It wasn’t that I lacked aptitude. I’d picked a language with a steep, frontloaded learning curve that’s genuinely better suited to people who already understand programming fundamentals. I just didn’t know that at the time, because nobody tells beginners that the language matters less than you’d think — except in the very specific way it matters enormously for your first few months.
I came back to coding two years later. Different language. Completely different experience. I want to walk through what actually changed, and what the current data says beginners should do differently than I did.

Why the “First Language” Decision Matters More Than People Admit
There’s a popular opinion in programming communities that the first language doesn’t matter much — that once you understand core concepts, you can pick up any language. That’s true eventually. It’s not true for the first eight weeks.
The first language determines whether you get fast, visible feedback on your effort or whether you spend weeks fighting syntax before you see anything work. And that early feedback loop is doing more than teaching you to code. It’s teaching your brain whether this skill is something you can actually get good at.
This isn’t just motivational fluff. Educational psychologist Albert Bandura’s research on self-efficacy — the belief in your own capacity to succeed at a task — found that early, achievable successes are disproportionately important in determining whether someone persists with a new skill. Fail too often, too early, and the brain quietly concludes the activity isn’t for you. Succeed early and often, and persistence becomes much easier to sustain.
I didn’t fail at Java because I was incapable. I failed because the early feedback loop was brutal and I didn’t have the conceptual scaffolding to push through it yet.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The data shifted significantly in just one year — and it matters for what beginners should choose.
What the Current Data Actually Shows
I want to ground this in real numbers rather than opinion, because most “best language for beginners” articles are recycled takes from 2019 that nobody’s updated.
According to the Stack Overflow Developer Survey 2025, JavaScript leads usage among professional developers at 66%, with HTML/CSS at 61.9%, SQL at 58.6%, and Python close behind at 57.9%. But the more interesting number, for our purposes, is what happened between 2024 and 2025: Python’s adoption accelerated significantly, seeing a 7 percentage point increase in a single year — a jump that reflects its expanding role in AI, data science, and backend development specifically.
Looking specifically at how beginners are guided, the same survey data shows JavaScript topping the list of recommended languages for people learning to code, largely because it runs in any browser and provides immediate visual feedback — which is exactly the early-success mechanism I mentioned above.
But Python tells a different and, I’d argue, more compelling story for absolute beginners. Python now powers more than 80% of today’s machine learning and AI projects, and on the TIOBE Index — which tracks search and tutorial volume rather than just professional usage — Python ranked #1 in February 2026 with a 21.81% share, well ahead of C, C++, and Java.
The two languages, JavaScript and Python, are genuinely competing for the top beginner recommendation right now. Which one is right depends less on raw popularity and more on what you’re actually trying to build.

JavaScript vs. Python: The Decision That Actually Matters
Here’s where I think most “best language” articles fail beginners. They present a ranking as if there’s one correct answer, when the honest answer is: it depends on what excites you enough to keep going when it gets hard. Because it will get hard, regardless of which one you choose.
JavaScript makes sense if you want to see things happen. It runs directly in a browser. You can write ten lines of code and watch a button change color, a page update, an interactive element respond to a click. That visual, immediate payoff is enormously motivating in the first few weeks, which is exactly when motivation is most fragile.
Python makes sense if you’re drawn to data, automation, or AI. The syntax reads closer to plain English than almost any other widely used language, which reduces the early cognitive load significantly. You’re not fighting curly braces and semicolons while also trying to understand loops and variables for the first time. Once you’ve learned JavaScript, TypeScript becomes a natural next step — but Python doesn’t really have that “level two” requirement. It just keeps being useful as you go deeper.
I’ll admit a slight bias here, because Python was the language that actually worked for me on the second attempt. I’m aware that bias is worth naming rather than pretending I’m a neutral narrator.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Less mental effort spent on syntax means more left over for actually understanding logic.
The Readability Advantage Nobody Quantifies Well
There’s a specific cognitive reason Python tends to work better for true beginners, and it’s not just about “being friendlier.”
Programming requires holding multiple abstract concepts in working memory simultaneously — syntax rules, logical structure, the actual problem you’re trying to solve. Cognitive load theory, developed by educational psychologist John Sweller, suggests that when too much of that working memory is consumed by surface-level syntax, there’s less capacity left for the deeper conceptual learning that actually matters.
Python’s syntax was deliberately designed to minimize that surface-level burden. Indentation instead of brackets. Words instead of symbols where possible. The result is that beginners spend a higher proportion of their mental effort on logic and structure, and less on remembering where a semicolon goes.
That’s not a small thing. It’s the difference between spending your limited daily learning capacity on “how do loops work” versus “why won’t this compile.”

Where This Gets Complicated: Career Considerations
I don’t want to pretend the decision is purely pedagogical, because for a lot of people learning to code, the actual goal is employment.
JavaScript remains dominant in web development with a massive ecosystem built around React and Node.js, and front-end and full-stack roles built on JavaScript are abundant, especially for early-career developers. If your goal is “I want to build websites and get hired at a company doing that,” JavaScript is the more direct path.
Python, meanwhile, is the language most teams reach for first when scaling AI features, and senior Python developers are frequently the first hire for companies building in that space. If your interest leans toward data, machine learning, automation, or backend systems, Python’s trajectory is arguably stronger right now given how central it’s become to the AI boom.
Neither path is wrong. But going in with a sense of which direction interests you more will save you the kind of detour I took with Java — a technically respectable language that simply wasn’t the right entry point for what I actually wanted to do.

What I’d Tell Beginners Now (Including Past Me)
If I were starting over today, knowing what the current data shows and what I’ve learned about how the early learning curve actually works, here’s roughly how I’d think about it.
If the idea of building something visual — a website, an interactive page, something you can show a friend within your first week — is what excites you, start with JavaScript. The immediate feedback loop will carry you through the early frustration in a way that more abstract languages won’t.
If you’re drawn to the idea of working with data, automating tedious tasks, or eventually touching AI and machine learning, start with Python. The gentler syntax will protect your motivation during the weeks when everything feels harder than it should.
What I wouldn’t recommend, unless you already have a specific reason, is starting with a heavily structured, verbose language like Java or C++ as your very first language. They’re excellent languages. They’re just not optimized for the particular psychological challenge of staying motivated as a total beginner, and that challenge — not raw intelligence — is what actually determines whether most people stick with coding long enough to get good at it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The language matters less than whether you show up again tomorrow.
The Honest Caveat
I want to end on something less tidy than a clean recommendation, because I think it matters.
The language you start with will not determine whether you become a good programmer. Plenty of people start with the “wrong” language for their goals and do fine. Plenty of people start with the statistically “right” language and quit anyway, because the actual obstacle was never syntax — it was consistency, or unclear goals, or simply life getting in the way.
What the data can tell you is which language reduces unnecessary early friction. It can’t tell you whether you’ll show up tomorrow. That part’s still on you.
I wasted four months on the wrong language for me. I don’t think that time was wasted in some deeper sense — I learned things about persistence and about how I learn that I wouldn’t have otherwise. But if a clearer starting point could have saved me those four months, I’d have taken it. That’s what I’m trying to offer here.

If you’re starting to learn programming, or thinking about it, I’d love to hear in the comments what’s pulling you toward it — building websites, working with data, something else entirely. It might help clarify which path makes sense for you. And if this kind of practical, research-backed writing is useful, follow me here for more.
